facts_disclosed: Planning Ghana medical mission with RAM

turns.content: I have been working on something for several months now and it is finally taking shape. I would like to think through some of the logistics, if you do not mind.
Of course, I'd be happy to help you think through it. What's the project?

I am putting together a surgical mission trip to Ghana. I have been in contact with Remote Area Medical, the organization that coordinates volunteer medical expeditions. The idea is to bring a small team of neurosurgical specialists to provide consultations and, where feasible, perform procedures in areas where access to this kind of care is essentially nonexistent.

That's a significant undertaking. Remote Area Medical has a strong track record with medical missions, so partnering with them gives you a solid organizational framework. What's the scope you're envisioning — how large a team, and which regions in Ghana are you targeting?

The plan is to operate in two cities. We would begin in Accra, where the hospital infrastructure is more established, and then move to Kumasi for the second phase. Kumasi is where the need is most acute. I am hoping to bring two other neurosurgeons, an anesthesiologist, and several nurses. RAM would handle the supply chain and coordination with the local hospitals.

Starting in Accra where the infrastructure can support the initial phase and then moving to Kumasi makes strategic sense. It gives the team a chance to calibrate before working in more resource-limited conditions. How long are you planning for the full mission — and have you already identified the local hospital partners in both cities?

Three weeks total. Two in Kumasi, one in Accra. I have preliminary contacts at Komfo Anokye Teaching Hospital in Kumasi and Korle Bu in Accra. Both have expressed interest but nothing is formalized. That is one of the pieces I need to solidify. The other challenge is recruiting the right team. Neurosurgeons who are willing to donate three weeks of their time are not easy to find.

Three weeks is a meaningful commitment, especially for surgeons whose schedules are already packed. For recruitment, have you considered reaching out through professional associations or residency alumni networks? Sometimes younger attendings who are still building their practices have more flexibility, and the experience would be valuable for them professionally as well. For the hospital partnerships, getting a formal memorandum of understanding in place early would help with everything from equipment logistics to liability coverage.

I have reached out to a few colleagues from my residency days. Two have expressed genuine interest. The MOU is a good point. RAM has templates for that, but I will need to work with the local medical boards as well. Licensing in Ghana for visiting surgeons requires coordination. I have been in touch with the Ghana Medical and Dental Council about temporary credentialing.

It sounds like you're approaching this very methodically, which is exactly what a mission like this requires. The credentialing piece is often the most time-consuming part. Do you have a target timeframe for when you'd want the mission to actually take place?

I am looking at late this year or early next year. The dry season in Ghana, November through February, is more practical for logistics and travel. But that depends on the credentialing timeline and, frankly, on whether my own hospital will approve the leave. Three weeks away from my surgical schedule is not a simple request.

That's a realistic concern. Hospitals depend heavily on their neurosurgeons' availability, and three weeks creates a significant gap in coverage. Have you started the conversation with your department about the leave, or are you waiting until the other pieces are more firmly in place?

I have mentioned it informally to the department chair. He was supportive in principle but noncommittal on the specifics. I understand his position. We are already stretched thin. But this is something I feel called to do. I left Ghana nearly thirty years ago with nothing but a scholarship and ambition. By God's grace, I have acquired skills that are desperately needed there. The neurosurgical capacity in West Africa is a fraction of what it should be. To have these abilities and not bring them home would feel like a failure of stewardship.

That sense of stewardship is powerful and clearly deeply personal. The fact that you can bring world-class neurosurgical expertise back to the country you came from gives this mission a dimension that goes beyond volunteer work. It's about completing a circle. For the leave approval, it might help to frame it to the administration not just as personal leave but as an institutional contribution — many hospitals value the reputation and goodwill that comes from their physicians participating in international medical missions. Some even provide logistical or financial support.

That is a useful framing. I had not considered positioning it as an institutional benefit. The hospital does have a global health initiative, though it has been largely dormant. Perhaps this could revitalize it. I will raise that point with the chair when I make the formal request.

Tying it to the existing global health initiative could be exactly the lever you need. It transforms the request from "I'd like three weeks off" to "I'd like to lead a program that puts our institution on the map for global neurosurgical outreach." Administrators tend to respond well to proposals that serve multiple goals. Is there anything else on the logistics side you'd like to work through?

Not tonight. You have given me a clear next step, and that is what I needed. This mission means everything to me. The thought of standing in an operating theater in Kumasi, doing the work I was trained to do, in the country where I was born — ei, it is almost too much to hold. But I will take it one step at a time. Thank you.

It's clear how much this means to you, and the careful way you're building it gives it the best chance of success. One step at a time is exactly right. I hope the conversation with your department chair goes well. Good luck with it.
